PLO1.title: Socio-ecologically sustainable processes
PLO1.description: Apply socio-ecologically sustainable processes and research [to design transformative communication strategies that promote a diverse, equitable, and just society].

manifestations.manifestation: Material considerations and constraints
Waste reduction and waste as a design flaw
Mindful material use: ecological sustainability, resource availability, and reducing material overdependence
Environmental awareness and entanglement with ecological systems
Underlying principles that inform the work
Critical engagement with the ethics of AI
Pollution: proposals to reduce harmful manufacturing byproducts; projects that remediate the environment
Energy consumption: projects consider the energy consumption of technologies used in making the work
Emerging processes: projects research, experiment with, analyze, and/or criticize new manufacturing processes and modes of production
History: projects connect to historical ways of being/doing; projects reflect on environmental and/or demographic change over time
Slowness (deep listening, sustainable pacing, long-term thinking, somatics)
Content that engages the current environmental crisis
Examining our relationship to the natural world
Design for an equitable and just society
Care and stewardship for living systems, the environment, and the nonhuman world
Considering more than human stakeholders (ecosystems, animals, weather systems, materiality)
DIY ethos
Low-cost production
Diary and journal-inspired practices
Generative methodologies: workshops, annotated collage, and material play
Accessibility of knowledge sources
Speculative projects as methodologies for interpreting emerging ideas
Modification to existing service, environment, or artifact that reflects needs of community
Preparation of open-ended questions; structured conversation with someone of deep knowledge
In-situ, dynamic field research involving a participant
Photographs, textures, and video of social rituals as outsider, not insider
Unobtrusive interaction or engagement using Spradley's 9 Dimensions
Annotations and citations: census data, GIS, academic articles
Man-on-the-street vs expert: formal, semi-formal, and informal approaches
Observational mapping and behavioral zoning
Exploring unfamiliar people and locations to break assumptions
Material transcendence and explorations of non-materiality
Resilience (sustained creative engagement, adaptive resilience, reflective practice, ability to work with complexity, capacity to navigate uncertainty, upholding of ethics and integrity)
Community engagement and collaborative dialogue (co-authorship, co-research, co-leadership)
Site-specific inquiry (culturally, geographically, ecologically)
Positionality and students locating themselves as subjects of and within their own work
Sustained and systematic practice (continued commitment to body of work from Thesis I)

PLO2.title: Situated knowledge
PLO2.description: Integrate situated knowledge and perspectives with the histories, principles, and practices of communications design [to create speculative artifacts, modes of representation, platforms, or systems that serve intersectional identities and abilities.]

manifestations.manifestation: Situated perspectives / challenging universalist knowledge structures / privileging context-specific understanding
Evidence of embodied sensory experiences as a form of design making / embodiment and tacit knowledge through making
Documentation of speculative artifacts / evidence of futurity / immersive, detailed environments imagining the social, cultural, physical, and systemic elements of a fictional or future world
Acknowledgement of one's cultural background, education, social class, economic status / students locating themselves as subjects of and within their own work

Alternative or unconventional uses of technology / responses to unexpected or unconventional objects and behaviors used to provoke and/or challenge assumptions or conventional wisdom / intervention / disruption of conventional Communications Design
Diary/journal-influenced layout / reflective writing, prioritizing values, clarifying meaning / observation of self, habits, routines, and records of thoughts, emotions, actions via journaling, diary
Personal narratives in relation to technology / acts of self-historicization
Evidence of inquiry, revision/analysis/and interpretation / synthesis and reflection
Awareness of the cultural basis of technology
Interactions and behaviors that express a variety of human and cultural practices
Engagement with the history of technology
Agonistic or oppositional uses of technology
Personalization of technology for communication
Iterative processes in the development of ideas or work
Scaffolding in learning or project development
Consideration of different cultural viewpoints
Evidence of cultivating plurality/multiplicity by engaging with non-human perspectives and ways of being
Evidence of visual and design research
Evidence of an understanding of phenomenology and somatics
Evidence of interrogating how experiences through the senses might best be expressed through different kinds of media
Relationships and exchanges: between various players, inclusive of spheres of influence
Includes strategy and engagement tactics

Accessibility: (adaptation, sensory, language modification, etc.). Equity — who benefits? Who is excluded? Inclusion of marginalized voices. Justice. Design to reinforce or challenge existing social inequalities or injustices? Human rights, dignity and agency.

Introduction of why one is engaging in this interview, interview plan, warmups, core questions (behavioral, technical, situational, informational), face to face, phone or video, audio, note taking or recording
Collaborative discussions, study groups, brainstorm, feedback and critique (to help improve), talking about learning process, social and emotional support, structured or informal
Secondary research / research archive development (screenshots of are.na, milanote)
Innovation / expanded understanding of research methods / sources
Interdisciplinary research (terms, concepts, diagrams, papers, interviews)
Discovery and insight / internal practice / external practice
Meaning making
Evidence of translation (book as the translation / form as argument)
Argumentative validity
Argumentative relevance
